Buckley Diversion Dam; United States, Washington, Pierce County; Collected: 1-Dec-1954; Remark: A freshwater Cottus. ID of C. rhotheus by KMC based on big head, complete lateral line, ray counts, but has more black on membranes of first dorsal fin than I see in most photos. Needs examination by someone more familiar with Cottus.

Big Lake; United States, Washington, Skagit County; Collected: 1-Jul-1952; Remark: Original ID of A. fulvescens, Lake Sturgeon, which occurs east of the the Rockies. As far as I can tell, it is a White Sturgeon, but the museum has few sturgeons and no Lake Sturgeons for comparison. White Sturgeons do not currently occur in Big Lake.

Paradise Creek; United States, Washington, Whitman County; Collected: 12-Oct-1948; Remark: Determined to be C. columbianus by R. R. Miller in 1950, but has deep cleft in lower lip.. KM Cassidy, 2019, thinks it looks more like C. macrocheilus (deep cleft in lower lip; scales appear to increase in size toward tail), but specimen is very battered. Many scales obscured by fungal growth; dorsal fins partly folded and fragile.  Low confidence in ID.

Fish accidently put in jar with the wrong field tag?

Between two of the Cone Islands, near Eagle Bay, Cypress Island; United States, Washington, Skagit County; Collected: 12-Aug-1950; Remark: ID by KM Cassidy, Oct, 2020. Color faded but 5 vertical black bars make this an unusually distinctive rockfish. High confidence.

Elliot Bay; United States, Washington, King County; Collected: 17-Apr-1956; Remark: ID notes for 21-343 to -347. These 5 fish IDed by collectors as "juvenile Sebastodes." ID of Copper Rockfish by KMCassidy, Oct 2020. Low/moderate confidence. Preservation has removed most of the red and yellow pigment useful in Rockfish ID. ID was based on head spines (5 pairs), concave/flat space between eyes, fin ray counts, the likelihood of the species at that location, and the faint color pattern that could be deduced. Quillback Rockfish also possible, but couldn't see any remnant of black speckling on throat and dorsal fin spines don't seem incised enough. Need examination by a rockfish expert.

Rosario Bay, off Rosario Head and Northwest Island; United States, Washington, Skagit County; Collected: 1-Jul-1959; Remark: ID to genus by collectors; ID to species by KM Cassidy, Oct 2020. High confidence in genus, low conf in species. In favor of S. variabilis: no head spines, has pale area at base of anal fins and low pectoral, dorsal spine and ray count consistent. Against: not common so far south; usually in deeper water, has more mottling and faint images of patches than illustrated in guides. Needs examination by someone more experienced in rockfish ID.

Kona Coast; United States, Hawaii, Hawaii County; Collected: August, 1971; Remark: Collectors wrote "Unidentified" on field tag. Attempted ID by KM Cassidy Nov 2020, little headway, Fins fragile, tightly folded, color mostly faded. In August 2021, sent photos to Luke Torrebene (Burke). He recognized as probable juvenile Coryphaena hippurus. Looks like match base on body and mouth shape and vertical barring.
